3893	\begin{proof}
3894	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp,
3895	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
3896	  This is the tensorator analog of the unit-comparison composition coherence
3897	  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}, and is proved by the same mate calculus rather
3898	  than on sections (the left-adjoint pullback exposes no sectionwise value). The oplax
3899	  comparison \(\delta\) of a composite of left adjoints decomposes through the
3900	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism: Mathlib's
3901	  \(\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp\_}\delta\) expresses the \(\delta\) of a composite
3902	  oplax monoidal functor as the whiskered composite of the \(\delta\)'s of the factors.
3903	  Applying this to the two factorisations of the underlying functor of the square
3904	  \(f \circ j' = j \circ g\) --- as \((j')^* \circ f^*\) and as \(g^* \circ j^*\) ---
3905	  and conjugating the pullback pseudofunctoriality isomorphisms by the
3906	  adjunction mate correspondence (\(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) for the left adjoints
3907	  corresponds to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) for the right adjoints, via
3908	  \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\)) identifies the two restricted
3909	  comparisons. The restriction along the open immersion \(j'\) is the strong-monoidal
3910	  restriction functor of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so it carries
3911	  \(\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) to its value on the restricted modules without
3912	  introducing a defect; the displayed identification of
3913	  \((j')^*\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) with \(\delta^{g}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) follows.
3914	  The final clause is then immediate, an isomorphism being preserved and reflected by the
3915	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism it is conjugated by.
3916	\end{proof}

5503	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
5504	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
5505	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules

5514	\begin{proof}
5515	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5516	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5517	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5518	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5519	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5520	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5521	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5522	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5523	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5524	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5525	
5526	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5527	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
5528	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
5529	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
5530	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
5531	  \[
5532	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
5533	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
5534	  \]
5535	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
5536	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
5537	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
5538	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
5539	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
5540	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
5541	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
5542	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
5543	
5544	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
5545	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
5546	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
5547	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
5548	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
5549	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
5550	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
5551	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
5552	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
5553	  \emph{exactly}
5554	  \[
5555	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
5556	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5557	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
5558	  \]
5559	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
5560	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
5561	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
5562	  two-leg split of the whole step.
5563	
5564	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
5565	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5566	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
5567	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
5568	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
5569	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
5570	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
5571	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
5572	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
5573	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
5574	
5575	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5576	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5577	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5578	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5579	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5580	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5581	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5582	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5583	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5584	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
5585	
5586	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5587	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5588	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5589	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
5590	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5591	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5592	
5593	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5594	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5595	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5596	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5597	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5598	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5599	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5600	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5601	
5602	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5603	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5604	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5605	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5606	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5607	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5608	
5609	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5610	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5611	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5612	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5613	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5614	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5615	
5616	  \emph{An alternative route}, not committed to here, derives the isomorphism from the
5617	  already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of monoidal inverses:
5618	  the dual is the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and restriction commutes with
5619	  \(\otimes\), so evaluation/coevaluation naturality transports the inverse. This sidesteps
5620	  leg (A) entirely, and is a candidate should the inverse-uniqueness glue prove cheaper than
5621	  building the slice-site base-change directly.
5622	\end{proof}

5624	\begin{lemma}\leanok
5625	[Dual of the structure sheaf]
5626	  \label{lem:dual_unit_iso}
5627	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso}
5628	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5629	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5630	  structure sheaf itself,
5631	  \[
5632	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
5633	  \]
5634	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
5635	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
5636	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
5637	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5638	\end{lemma}
5639	
5640	\begin{proof}
5641	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
5642	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
5643	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
5644	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
5645	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
5646	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
5647	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
5648	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
5649	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
5650	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
5651	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
5652	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
5653	\end{proof}

5675	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
5676	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual

5681	\begin{proof}
5682	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso,
5683	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5684	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
5685	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
5686	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
5687	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
5688	  \[
5689	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
5690	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
5691	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
5692	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
5693	  \]
5694	  \begin{enumerate}
5695	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
5696	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
5697	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
5698	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
5699	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
5700	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
5701	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
5702	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
5703	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
5704	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
5705	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
5706	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\cref{lem:dual_unit_iso}):
5707	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
5708	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
5709	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
5710	  \end{enumerate}
5711	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
5712	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
5713	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
5714	\end{proof}

5792	  component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given component of \(f_i\), conjugated
5793	  by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications arising from the open-set equality
5794	  \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) valid for \(V \subseteq U_i\). The non-trivial content is

5798	  automatic on the thin poset of opens of \(X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This

5803	\begin{proof}
5804	  \uses{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
5805	  Fix \(i\). The section \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}(U_i)\) is a morphism of
5806	  the restrictions of the underlying abelian-group presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), indexed
5807	  over the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). We define its component at a pair
5808	  \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) by conjugating the given component of \(f_i\): writing
5809	  \(V' = \iota_i^{-1}(V)\) for the preimage of \(V\) under the open immersion
5810	  \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\), take the section map \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V') \colon M(V') \to N(V')\)
5811	  of the underlying ab-presheaf morphism of \(f_i\), and conjugate it on the source by the
5812	  restriction \(M(\mathtt{eqToHom})\) and on the target by \(N(\mathtt{eqToHom})\) along the
5813	  down-set identity \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) (valid for \(V \subseteq U_i\)).
5814	  The result is a morphism \(M(V) \to N(V)\).
5815	
5816	  The substantive content is the \emph{naturality} of this assignment across a morphism
5817	  \(\varphi\) of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\), say from a pair \((B)\) to a pair \((A)\)
5818	  with underlying inclusion \(B \subseteq A\). Unwinding the two composite routes, one must
5819	  show --- separately on the source presheaf \(M\) and the target presheaf \(N\) --- that the
5820	  \(\varphi\)-restriction followed by the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) at \(B\) equals the
5821	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) at \(A\) followed by the \(\kappa\)-restriction, where
5822	  \(\kappa \colon \iota_i^{-1}(B) \to \iota_i^{-1}(A)\) is the induced inclusion of
5823	  preimages. Each of these two equations is an equality of parallel morphisms between the
5824	  same pair of opens in the thin poset \((\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}}\), so it holds by
5825	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); combined with the naturality of the underlying ab-presheaf
5826	  morphism of \(f_i\) across \(\kappa\), the two routes agree. This is precisely the
5827	  section-direction slice of the comparison packaged in
5828	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}.

5839	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
5840	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
5841	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
5842	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5843	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
5844	\end{lemma}

5846	\begin{proof}
5847	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
5848	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
5849	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
5850	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
5851	
5852	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
5853	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
5854	  \(\mathcal{H}\) (\(\mathtt{presheafHom}\) of the underlying presheaves of abelian groups
5855	  of \(M\) and \(N\)), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
5856	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
5857	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
5858	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
5859	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
5860	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
5861	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
5862	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
5863	  strictly more bookkeeping.
5864	
5865	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
5866	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}(U_i)\)
5867	  of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_hom_local_section},
5868	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
5869	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
5870	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
5871	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
5872	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
5873	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality condition} of
5874	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
5875	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
5876	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece to be
5877	  built and verified first, as a standalone lemma.
5878	
5879	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
5880	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
5881	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
5882	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
5883	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
5884	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
5885	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
5886	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
5887	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This A-engine uses the
5888	  sheaf-site equivalence \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}; the C-bridge
5889	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, by contrast, uses a per-open slice comparison at
5890	  the presheaf level over the \emph{varying} section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), and so
5891	  does \emph{not} reduce to the same sheaf-site root --- the two are independent and
5892	  must be built separately.
5893	
5894	  Of the remaining four sub-steps, three are mechanical and one --- the cocycle bridge
5895	  (a) --- carries the genuine content. We spell it out.
5896	
5897	  \emph{(a) The cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
5898	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\).} The condition \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) asks that for
5899	  every pair of indices \(i, j\) the two restrictions of \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\) and
5900	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,j\) to the overlap coincide as sections of the hom-sheaf
5901	  \(\mathcal{H}\) over \(U_i \sqcap U_j\), i.e.\ an honest equality in the single abelian
5902	  group \(\mathcal{H}(U_i \sqcap U_j)\). We must therefore identify the overlap-agreement
5903	  hypothesis on the family \((f_i)_i\) that furnishes this. The correct hypothesis --- and
5904	  the only one a caller can actually produce --- is the \emph{sectionwise} one:
5905	
5906	  \begin{quote}
5907	    for all indices \(i, j\) and every open \(V\) with \(V \subseteq U_i\) and
5908	    \(V \subseteq U_j\), the two section maps \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V)\) and
5909	    \((f_j).\mathtt{app}(V)\) agree as morphisms in the single, fixed abelian-group hom-type
5910	    \(M(V) \to N(V)\), the value of the hom-presheaf \(\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) at
5911	    \(V\).
5912	  \end{quote}
5913	
5914	  This is exactly the data a caller has at hand: two local morphisms that literally agree on
5915	  every open contained in both \(U_i\) and \(U_j\).
5916	
5917	  It is worth contrasting this with a naive alternative that does \emph{not} work. One might
5918	  try to compare the two double-restrictions \((f_i)|_{U_i \cap U_j}\) and
5919	  \((f_j)|_{U_i \cap U_j}\) directly, as morphisms reached by the two slice-restriction
5920	  routes. But these two morphisms do not live in a common type at all: each is the image of
5921	  \(f\) under the pullback-of-modules functor along a \emph{different} slice-restriction
5922	  map, and the resulting source and target objects are sheafifications of pullback presheaves
5923	  along different morphisms. Such objects are only \emph{isomorphic}, through a nontrivial
5924	  comparison isomorphism, and are \emph{not} propositionally equal. A heterogeneous
5925	  comparison of the two double-restrictions therefore cannot be reduced to an honest
5926	  equation --- there is no object equality to transport along, so no
5927	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) of the thin poset and no heterogeneous-to-homogeneous
5928	  elimination applies --- and, crucially, no caller can produce such a datum in the first
5929	  place. The sectionwise hypothesis sidesteps this entirely by comparing only the section
5930	  maps, which live in the fixed group \(M(V) \to N(V)\).
5931	
5932	  With the sectionwise hypothesis in hand the passage to \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) is
5933	  \emph{direct}. Restrict \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\) to the overlap \(U_i \sqcap U_j\). By
5934	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_hom_local_section}, the component of \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\)
5935	  at an open \(V \subseteq U_i\) is precisely \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V')\), where
5936	  \(V' = \iota_i^{-1}(V)\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
5937	  arising from the down-set identity \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\); restricting
5938	  to the overlap simply evaluates this component at the opens \(V \subseteq U_i \sqcap U_j\),
5939	  and likewise for \(j\). Thus both restrictions, evaluated at such a \(V\), are conjugates
5940	  of \((f_i).\mathtt{app}(V')\) and \((f_j).\mathtt{app}(V')\) by \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)
5941	  morphisms, all lying in the fixed group \(M(V) \to N(V)\). The sectionwise hypothesis
5942	  equates the two middle terms \((f_i).\mathtt{app}\) and \((f_j).\mathtt{app}\) directly.
5943	  The only remaining identification is that the two \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation routes
5944	  --- built from the two down-set identities, one for \(U_i\) and one for \(U_j\) --- agree;
5945	  since both are parallel morphisms in the thin poset \((\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}}\),
5946	  they coincide by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). Hence the two restricted local sections
5947	  agree in \(\mathcal{H}(U_i \sqcap U_j)\), which is exactly \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\).
5948	
5949	  Note the division of labour: the substantive content is the sectionwise agreement of the
5950	  \(f_i\), an equation in fixed abelian-group hom-types that a caller can supply; the
5951	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation and the \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) collapse of the
5952	  thin-poset routes are the only mechanical identifications, and they are legitimate
5953	  precisely because they act on the thin poset of opens of \(X\), never on the genuinely
5954	  distinct \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-hom-set section maps.
5955	
5956	  Two of the remaining sub-steps are mechanical: (b) gluing via
5957	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
5958	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g\) of the underlying presheaves of
5959	  \(M\) and \(N\) --- the
5960	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
5961	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
5962	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
5963	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
5964	  of the \(\top\)-section; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
5965	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
5966	
5967	  Sub-step (c) --- the sectionwise \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity check that promotes the
5968	  glued morphism \(g\) of underlying ab-sheaves to a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
5969	  --- is, by contrast, \emph{not} mechanical. It is the point at which the several module
5970	  structures in play (the native section-ring action of \(\mathcal{O}_X\), and the
5971	  restriction-of-scalars action induced by the open immersions) must be reconciled. The
5972	  required identity \(g(r \cdot x) = r \cdot g(x)\), checked sectionwise over an open
5973	  \(P\), splits into three legs.
5974	  \begin{itemize}
5975	    \item \emph{(c.i) The \(M\)-leg (source semilinearity).} Restriction of a section
5976	      against a structure-ring scalar is discharged by the native, \(\Gamma\)-module-level
5977	      functoriality identity \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.map\_smul}\,M\): for a restriction
5978	      map \(i\) one has

5980	      section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X\), with \emph{no} restriction-of-scalars artifact. This
5981	      leg closes cleanly.

5984	      \emph{restriction-of-scalars} action carried by \((M|_{\iota_i})(P)\) --- restriction
5985	      of scalars along the structure-ring map of the open immersion \(\iota_i\) --- whereas
5986	      the \(f\)-leg of the goal feeds in the \emph{native}
5987	      \(\mathcal{O}_X(\mathrm{image})\)-module action. These two actions are
5988	      propositionally equal but \emph{not} definitionally equal. The reconciliation is the
5989	      genuine content of (c): for an open immersion the structure-ring comparison
5990	      isomorphism is the identity --- \(\mathtt{appIso}(\iota_i) = \mathtt{Iso.refl}\)
5991	      --- so the ring map along which scalars are restricted is the identity \(\mathbf{1}\);
5992	      and restriction of scalars along the \emph{identity} ring map returns the original
5993	      action,
5994	      \(c \cdot_{\,\mathrm{restr}\,\mathbf{1}} x = \mathbf{1}(c) \cdot x = c \cdot x\),
5995	      identified by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars.smul\_def}\) together with
5996	      \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsId'App}\). This identity-ring-map smul bridge --- the
5997	      localization observation that restriction of scalars along an isomorphism that
5998	      happens to be the identity reproduces the native module action --- is the substantive
5999	      mathematics of sub-step (c).
6000	    \item \emph{(c.iii) The \(N\)-leg (target semilinearity).} Once the bridge of (c.ii) is
6001	      in place, the target leg is discharged by the native identity
6002	      \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.map\_smul}\,N\), exactly as in (c.i); the scalars transported
6003	      across the two legs then reconcile because the two open-set \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) maps
6004	      arising from the down-set identity \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(W)\bigr) = W\) compose
6005	      to the identity on the overlap.
6006	  \end{itemize}

6008	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
6009	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
6010	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
6011	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
6012	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
6013	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
6014	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
6015	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
6016	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
6017	
6018	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
6019	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality condition and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
6020	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
6021	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
6022	  building that sheaf-site equivalence, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be

6024	  naturality, built as a standalone verified lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
6025	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
6026	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the stalk-tensor commutation.

28	\section{Setup and motivation}
29	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
30	
31	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
32	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
33	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
34	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
35	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
36	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
37	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
38	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
39	
40	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
41	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
42	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
43	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
44	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
45	\[
46	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
47	\]
48	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
49	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
50	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
51	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
52	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
53

13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
15	
16	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
17	organised around the relative Picard functor
18	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
19	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
20	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
21	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
22	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
23	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
24	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
25	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
26	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
27	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
28	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

1	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
2	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
3	
4	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
5	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the

8	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
9	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
10	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
11	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
12	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
13	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a

18	
19	\section{Setup and motivation}
20	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
21	
22	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
23	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
24	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
25	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
26	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
27	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
28	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
29	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
30	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
31	
32	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
33	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
34	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
35	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
36	chapters.
37	
38	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
39	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
40	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
41	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
42	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
43	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
44	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
45	
46	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
47	\label{sec:coh_flat}
48	
49	\begin{definition}
50	
51	\leanok
52	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
53	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
54	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
55	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
56	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
57	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
58	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
59	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
61	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
62	  passim.}
63	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
64	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
65	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
66	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
67	  \(A \to B\)).
68	\end{definition}
69	
70	\begin{remark}
71	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
72	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
73	  pulling back along the cartesian square
74	  \[
75	    \begin{tikzcd}
76	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
77	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
78	    \end{tikzcd}
79	  \]

31	\section{Setup and motivation}
32	\label{sec:quot_setup}
33	
34	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
35	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
36	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
37	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
38	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
39	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
40	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
41	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
42	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
43	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
44	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.

34	\section{Setup and motivation}
35	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
36	
37	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
38	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
39	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
40	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
41	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
42	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
43	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
44	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
45	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
46	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
47	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
48	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
49	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
50	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
51	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
52	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.

62	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
63	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
64	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
65	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
66	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
67	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
68	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).

28	\section{Setup and motivation}
29	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
30	
31	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
32	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
33	(\cref{def:genus}). By
34	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
35	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
36	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
37	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
38	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
39	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
40	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
41	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
42	degree of a line bundle.
43	
44	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
45	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
46	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
47	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
48	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
49	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
50	
51	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
52	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
53	
54	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
55	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
56	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
57	Lean substrate
58	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
59	outside the Picard context.

26	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
27	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
28	
29	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
30	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
31	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
32	equivalently (and functorially) the set
33	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
34	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
35	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
36	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
37	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
38	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
39	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).

45	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
46	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,

21	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
22	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
23	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
24	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
25	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
26	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
27	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
28	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
29	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
30	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
31	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
32	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
33	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
34	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
35	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
36	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
37	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
38	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
39	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to

42	\section{Setup and motivation}
43	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
44	
45	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
46	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
47	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
48	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
49	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
50	
51	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
52	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
53	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
54	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
55	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
56	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
57	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
58

2	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and

5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	
8	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
9	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
10	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
11	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
12	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
13	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
14	out of scope here.
15	
16	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
17	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
18	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
19	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
20	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
21	\[
22	  \begin{array}{ccc}
23	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
24	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
25	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
26	  \end{array}
27	\]
28	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
29	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
30	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
31

0	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
1	\label{chap:Genus}
2	
3	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
4	
5	\section{Definition}
6	
7	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
8	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
9	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
10	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
11	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
12	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
13	\begin{definition}\leanok
14	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
15	  \label{def:genus}
16	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
17	  
18	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
19	  \[
20	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
21	  \]
22	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
23	\end{definition}
24	
25	\begin{proof}
26	  
27	  \leanok
28	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
29	
30	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
31	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
32	    \[
33	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
34	    \]
35	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
36	  \end{remark}
37	
38	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
39	\end{proof}
40	
41	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
42	\begin{itemize}
43	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
44	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
45	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
46	\end{itemize}
47	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.

16	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field \(k\) is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
17	
18	\begin{quote}
19	  \emph{Let \(A\) be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant.}
20	\end{quote}
21	
22	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-\(0\) universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of \(f \colon C \to A\) when \(C\) has genus \(0\) and a \(k\)-rational marked point.
23	
24	\paragraph{Disposition (iter-155): \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is a gated NAMED GAP.} The named declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} is landed with a \texttt{sorry} body and is held as a \textbf{named gap} in the same family as \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Jacobian.tex) and the deferred Serre-duality / Riemann--Roch stack --- honestly disclosed, not on a prover lane. The substantive content (the C.2.d sub-step below, ruling out a \(1\)-dimensional image of \(f\)) reduces to the vanishing of the cotangent pull-back \(\mathrm df \colon f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\), and a decisive iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that \textbf{producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is irreducibly a global-sections fact}, gated on two genuinely-absent inputs:
25	
26	\begin{itemize}
27	  \item \textbf{(gap i) Global cotangent-triviality of \(A\):} \(\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_A^{\oplus g}\), generated by translation-invariant differentials. The presheaf globalisation that would build this (\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) was \textbf{excised from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}. Estimated \(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC. Mathlib's only ``group cotangent is trivial'' idiom is the smooth-manifold \texttt{GroupLieAlgebra} stack, which does not port to schemes.
28	  \item \textbf{(gap ii) Serre duality \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)} for the genus-\(0\) curve. Given the global trivialisation of gap (i), \(\mathrm df \in H^0\bigl(C, \mathcal{H}om(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr) \cong \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\). Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf and no Riemann--Roch; the project's \texttt{Cohomology/*} stack computes \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (genus) but has \textbf{no \(H^0\)-vanishing lemma and no genus\(\leftrightarrow\Omega\) bridge}. The genus-\(0\) hypothesis \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) does \emph{not} yield \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) without the duality. Estimated \(\sim 3000\)--\(8000\) LOC.
29	\end{itemize}
30	
31	\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
32	
33	The project does \textbf{not} open a ``produce \(\mathrm df = 0\)'' prover lane until one of two candidate closure routes is built; the decision between them is a leading open strategic question and the chapter commits to neither:
34	\begin{itemize}
35	  \item \textbf{Route (a):} build gaps (i) \(+\) (ii) directly.
36	  \item \textbf{Route (b):} the dual-abelian-variety keystone --- ``morphisms \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) are constant because \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\)''. Earlier rejected as ``needs \(\Pic^0\)'', but since the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Route A) needs the \(\Pic^0\) engine \emph{regardless}, route (b) may amortise the shared machinery and be globally cheaper than a standalone Serre-duality build.
37	\end{itemize}
38	
39	\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.
40	
41	\textbf{The Lean declaration's signature now carries \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}} (matching the theorem's existing docstring, which already described \(\bar k\) as algebraically closed --- the hypothesis was simply absent from the formal statement). Prior strategy iterations had instead routed through base-change to \(\bar k\) followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c, \(\sim 4\)--\(8\) iter / \(300\)--\(500\) LOC); the iter-127 framing then claimed all three pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build over an \emph{arbitrary} base field, dropping descent entirely. The alg-closed pivot supersedes that framing for the rigidity theorem itself: rigidity is established over \(\bar k\), and the descent of the constancy conclusion to a general \(k\) is a short downstream consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (faithfully-flat covers are epimorphisms of schemes), with the detailed descent living in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter. The group-scheme cotangent-triviality pieces (i)/(iii) below remain genuinely intrinsic to the group scheme and are stated over a general base where that is sound; only the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (the KDM/constants core) requires the alg-closed base.

23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:ab_setup}
25	
26	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
27	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
28	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
29	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
30	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
31	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
32	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
33	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
34	so
35	\[
36	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
37	\]
38	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
39	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
40	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
41	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
42	
43	This chapter packages:
44	\begin{enumerate}
45	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
46	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
47	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
48	    (Definition~REF),
49	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma

31	\section{Setup and motivation}
32	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
33	
34	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
35	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
36	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
37	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
38	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
39	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
40	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
41	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
42	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
43	
44	These two invariants agree:
45	\[
46	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
47	\]
48	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
49	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the

0	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
1	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
2	
3	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
4	
5	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
6	
7	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
8	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
9	
10	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
11	\[
12	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
13	\]
14	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
15	
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
18	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  
21	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
22	  \[
23	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
24	  \]
25	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
26	\end{definition}
27	
28	\begin{proof}
29	  
30	  \leanok
31	  The body is a direct categorical composition mirroring the general sheaf-cohomology presheaf functor. Universe pinning is required because the morphism universe of the sheaf category lives one level above the object universe.
32	\end{proof}
33	
34	\begin{definition}\leanok
35	[Cohomology presheaf, \(H'\) flavor]
36	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
37	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf}
38	  
39	  In the setting of Definition~REF, let \(\mathcal F\) be a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf
40	  \[
41	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F) \;:\; C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat
42	  \]
43	  to be the evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
44	\end{definition}
45	
46	\begin{proof}
47

61	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
62	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
63	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
64	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
65	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
66	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
67	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
68	  sheafification of the presheaf
69	  \[
70	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
71	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
72	  \]
73	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
74	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
75	\end{definition}

114	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}

120	% Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
121	% is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
122	% quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
123	% $R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
124	% $\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
125	% the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
126	% If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
127	% $p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
128	% Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
129	% and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
130	% paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
131	% an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
132	% Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
133	% Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
134	% we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
135	% $$
136	% 0 \to
137	% g_*\mathcal{F} \to
138	% a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
139	% c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
140	% R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
141	% $$
142	% see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
143	% By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
144	% $R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
145	% $R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
146	% Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
147	% Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
148	% this implies that each of the sheaves
149	% $R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
150	% exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
151	% on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
152	% Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete."
153	\begin{proof}
154	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
155	  Quasi-coherence is local on \(S\), and forming higher direct images commutes
156	  with restriction to open subschemes, so we may assume \(S\) is affine; then
157	  \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We argue by the induction principle
158	  for quasi-compact opens of a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme: for a
159	  quasi-compact open \(U \subset X\) with \(a = f|_U\), let \(P(U)\) be the
160	  property that \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\) for every
161	  quasi-coherent module \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(U\) and every \(p \geq 0\); the
162	  desired statement is \(P(X)\).
163	
164	  \emph{Affine base.} If \(U\) is affine then \(a\) is an affine morphism, so
165	  \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) for \(p \geq 1\) by the relative affine vanishing of
166	  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}, while
167	  \(R^0 a_*\mathcal{G} = a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent because the pushforward
168	  of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
169	  quasi-coherent. Hence \(P(U)\) holds.
170	
171	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(U \subset X\) be quasi-compact open, \(V \subset X\)
172	  affine open, and suppose \(P(U)\), \(P(V)\), \(P(U \cap V)\) hold. Writing
173	  \(a = f|_U\), \(b = f|_V\), \(c = f|_{U \cap V}\), \(h = f|_{U \cup V}\), the
174	  relative Mayer--Vietoris sequence
175	  \[
176	    0 \to h_*\mathcal{G} \to a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)
177	      \to c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V}) \to R^1 h_*\mathcal{G} \to \cdots
178	  \]
179	  exhibits each \(R^p h_*\mathcal{G}\) as an extension built from quasi-coherent
180	  sheaves \(R^p a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U)\), \(R^p b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)\),
181	  \(R^p c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V})\): it sits between the cokernel of a map of
182	  quasi-coherent sheaves and the kernel of a map of quasi-coherent sheaves, hence
183	  is quasi-coherent. Thus \(P(U \cup V)\) holds. By the induction principle
184	  \(P(X)\) holds, proving the lemma.
185	\end{proof}

200	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG (``Relative affine vanishing'').}
201	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
202	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. If \(f\) is affine, then
203	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\).
204	\end{lemma}
205	
206	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
207	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
208	% the sheaf
209	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
210	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
211	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
212	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
213	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
214	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
215	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
216	% opens we win."
217	\begin{proof}
218	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
219	  By Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}, \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the
220	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),
221	  \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\). Since \(f\) is affine, the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) of
222	  any affine open \(V \subset S\) is affine. The higher cohomology of a
223	  quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme vanishes, so
224	  \(H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\) and all
225	  affine \(V\). As the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), the sheafification of a
226	  presheaf that vanishes on a basis is zero; hence \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for
227	  all \(i \geq 1\).
228	\end{proof}
